title: Quiz: Code-Based Crypto Security
description: Test your understanding of code-based cryptography security

questions.question: What is the best known classical attack against the syndrome decoding problem?
questions.explanation: BJMM (Becker-Joux-May-Meurer) is the best known classical ISD algorithm, achieving approximately 2^{0.097n} complexity. Shor's and Grover's are quantum algorithms.

questions.question: How does quantum computing affect ISD attacks?
questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm provides approximately quadratic speedup for ISD search steps. Work factor W becomes roughly √W, or equivalently, bit security is halved. This is similar to symmetric crypto.

questions.question: Why do binary Goppa codes resist structural attacks?

options.text: They lack distinguishable algebraic structure when disguised

questions.explanation: When disguised as G' = S·G·P, binary Goppa codes appear indistinguishable from random codes. Unlike Reed-Solomon codes, they lack exploitable algebraic structure for support splitting attacks.

questions.question: What is implicit rejection in decapsulation?

options.text: Returning a pseudorandom value instead of error signal

questions.explanation: Implicit rejection returns a deterministic pseudorandom value derived from the secret key and ciphertext when decoding fails, instead of signaling an error. This prevents attackers from detecting decoding failures.

questions.question: Which operation is NOT typically required to be constant-time?
questions.explanation: Public key transmission involves no secret data and doesn't need to be constant-time. Error decoding, secret comparisons, and implicit rejection all involve secrets and must be constant-time.

questions.question: For Classic McEliece with NIST Category 5 security, which parameter set is recommended?
questions.explanation: Category 5 requires 256-bit classical security. mceliece6688128 and mceliece8192128 meet this requirement. mceliece348864 is Category 1, mceliece460896 is Category 3.

questions.question: In Prange's basic ISD, what determines the success probability?

options.text: Whether the chosen k positions contain no errors
The size of the public key

questions.explanation: Prange's ISD succeeds when the randomly chosen k-position information set is error-free. The probability is C(n-k,t)/C(n,t), which is the chance all t errors fall in the remaining n-k positions.

questions.question: Python implementations can achieve true constant-time execution for cryptographic operations.
questions.explanation: False! Python's interpreter, garbage collection, and dynamic typing make constant-time guarantees impossible. Production constant-time code must be written in C or assembly with careful verification.
